title: Caro–Yuster Question on f(n)
statement_text: Is it true that $f(n) \geq n/2 - o(n)$?
context_text: For a graph $G$, let $f(G)$ be the largest integer $k$ for which there exist two vertex-disjoint induced subgraphs of $G$ each on $k$ vertices, both inducing the same number of edges, and let $f(n)$ be the minimum of $f(G)$ over all graphs on $n$ vertices. The trivial upper bound gives $f(n) \leq \lfloor n/2 \rfloor$, while previous lower bounds reached only $f(n) = \Omega(n^{1/2})$; Caro and Yuster asked whether $f(n) \geq n/2 - o(n)$.
paper_contribution: Proves Theorem 1.1: for every $\varepsilon > 0$ there exists $N = N(\varepsilon)$ such that $f(G) \geq n/2 - \varepsilon n$ for every graph $G$ on $n > N$ vertices, thereby establishing $f(n) \geq n/2 - o(n)$ and answering the question affirmatively; the remaining gap is $n/2 - o(n) \leq f(n) \leq n/2 - \Omega(\log\log n)$.
notes: PDF source. The question is fully resolved in the affirmative by this paper's main theorem. Section 5 of the paper is stated to contain additional open questions but its content was not included in the extracted text.
paper_title: Disjoint induced subgraphs of the same order and size
